title: practical english usage - Part 179
content:
- Although she was tired, she went to work.

One conjunction is enough to join two clauses - we do not normally use two.

5 one   conjunction         for   two    clauses OK,  I did it. - But I didn't mean to.

Afterthoughts may also begin with conjunctions.

perfectly correct when used appropriately.

sentence beginning with a conjunction in this way. In fact, the structure is Schoolchildren are sometimes told that it is wrong to write a one-clause This government has got to go. Before it does any more damage.

Writers and speakers can also separate clauses for emphasis.

‘ Why did you do that?' ‘ Because I felt like it.' ‘ When are you going to get up? ‘ When I'm ready.' example, in answers.

sometimes a conjunction and its clause can stand alone. This happens, for Normally a conjunction connects two clauses into one sentence. However, 4 conjunctions         in  separate       sentences Some things are never forgotten, once learnt.

I always feel better after talking to you.

instead of subjects and full verbs (►   115.6).

whenever, once and until) can often be followed by -ing forms or past participles Many conjunctions that express time relations (after, before, since, when, while, Once in bed, I read for twenty minutes and then turned out the light.

When in Rome, do as the Romans do. Cook slowly until ready.

If in doubt, wait and see. (= If you are in doubt...) I'll pay for you if necessary. (= ... if it is necessary.) expressions like if necessary.

subject and the verb be can often be dropped, especially in common fixed However, after if, when, while, until, once, unless and (al)though, a pronoun depressed.because didn't know...) not She was depressed, because she didn't know what to do. (     She was to do.) She was depressed and didn't know what to do. (= and she didn't know what clauses (►  276 for details), but not normally in a subordinate clause. (More natural not You'll find Coca-Cola wherever you go. (     ... wherever you will go.) infinitive, simple past instead of past perfect.

clauses - for example present instead of future, simple past instead of would + Verbs in subordinate clauses are often simpler in form than verbs in main I will pray that he wins.

- It is unlikely that he will win.

This discovery will mean that we spend less on food.

- This discovery means that we will spend less on food.

in subordinate clauses. After common words like tell, ask, idea, prepositions are often dropped before They were not at all interested in what I thought.

I may be able to come: it depends on which day you are holding the meeting.

We discussed the question of who to appoint as manager.
